perturbation.original_text: For a period of eighteen (18) months from the date hereof, neither COMPANY nor its subsidiaries will, directly or indirectly, solicit to hire or hire any (i) officers of XPLORE, (ii) management-level employees of XPLORE or its affiliates with whom COMPANY or its Representatives have had direct contact in connection with the evaluation or negotiation of a possible transaction, or (iii) employees of XPLORE or its affiliates with whom COMPANY or its Representatives is first made aware of in connection with any due diligence conducted in connection with the evaluation of a possible transaction.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of six (6) months from the date hereof, neither COMPANY nor its subsidiaries will, directly or indirectly, solicit to hire or hire any (i) officers of XPLORE, (ii) management-level employees of XPLORE or its affiliates with whom COMPANY or its Representatives have had direct contact in connection with the evaluation or negotiation of a possible transaction, or (iii) employees of XPLORE or its affiliates with whom COMPANY or its Representatives is first made aware of in connection with any due diligence conducted in connection with the evaluation of a possible transaction.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-solicitation period to 6 months may be considered an unreasonably short duration to protect trade secrets and confidential information under certain state laws, making it potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding non-compete agreements and reasonableness of duration
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware courts assess the reasonableness of non-compete agreements, including non-solicitation clauses, based on factors like duration and geographic scope. A six-month period might be deemed insufficient to protect legitimate business interests, especially in industries with long-term projects or sensitive client relationships, potentially conflicting with the requirement for reasonable restrictions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Article 7 Authenticated PDF Documents of Title Part 1. General Part 2. Warehouse Receipts: Special Provisions Part 3. Bills of Lading: Special Provisions Part 4. Warehouse Receipts and Bills of Lading: General Obligations Part 5. Warehouse Receipts and Bills of Lading: Negotiation and Transfer Part 6. Warehouse Receipts and Bills of Lading: Miscellaneous Provisions Part 7. Miscellaneous Provisions
perturbation.justification: The modification from 18 months to 6 months in the non-solicitation clause could be deemed insufficient to protect legitimate business interests, as stated in the Law Explanation. This aligns with the perturbation explanation which highlights the potential for the reduced period to be an unreasonably short duration to protect trade secrets. Although there is no direct contradiction with the scraped snippets due to their limited content, the Law Explanation suggests that Delaware courts assess the reasonableness of non-compete agreements based on factors like duration. The change weakens the original intent of the contract to protect business interests for a reasonable duration, thus contradicting legal standards for non-solicitation agreements.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties irrevocably submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the State of Delaware and the federal courts of the United States of America located in the State of Delaware over any dispute, claim or matter arising out of or relating to the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties irrevocably submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of the State of California over any dispute, claim or matter arising out of or relating to the Agreement, regardless of conflict of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the jurisdiction to California while maintaining Delaware law may cause conflict, as California courts may refuse to enforce a Delaware choice-of-law provision if it violates a fundamental California public policy or statute.
perturbation.law_explanation: While California courts often honor choice-of-law provisions, CCP § 410.30 allows them to stay or dismiss actions when a more convenient forum exists. By mandating California jurisdiction and excluding consideration of conflict of laws, the modified text could force litigation in California even if Delaware is a more appropriate forum under traditional conflict of law principles, thus creating a potential conflict with California procedural law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.30 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. (Amended by Stats. 1972, Ch. 601. Note: This version was suspended from Sept. 22, 1986, until Jan. 1, 1992, during operation of the temporary amendment by Stats. 1986, Ch. 968.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by mandating California jurisdiction while potentially applying Delaware law. This conflicts with California Code of Civil Procedure (CCP) § 410.30(a), which allows a California court to stay or dismiss an action if it finds that a forum outside the state would better serve the interests of substantial justice. The original text allows for Delaware jurisdiction, aligning with Delaware law. The changed text forces California jurisdiction while excluding consideration of conflict of laws, potentially overriding the court's ability to determine the most appropriate forum as outlined in CCP § 410.30(a), thereby creating a conflict with California procedural law.

perturbation.original_text: 13. The obligation of each of COMPANY and XPLORE to comply with the provisions contained herein shall continue for a period of four(4) years commencing upon the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. The obligation of each of COMPANY and XPLORE to comply with the provisions contained herein shall continue indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: An indefinite confidentiality period may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under Delaware law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding trade secrets and reasonableness of restrictive covenants.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Delaware protects trade secrets, an indefinite duration for confidentiality, especially for information beyond trade secrets, may be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade. Courts often require limitations on the duration of confidentiality obligations, particularly for non-trade secret information. The change could render the clause unenforceable as overly broad and against public policy promoting competition.

Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b
perturbation.justification: The modification changes the confidentiality period from a fixed term of four years to an indefinite term. The Perturbation Explanation states that an indefinite confidentiality period may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under Delaware law. The Law Explanation supports this, suggesting it could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines trade secrets under Delaware law (Title 6, Chapter 20, Â§ 2001), and the Law Explanation highlights that indefinite confidentiality, especially for information beyond trade secrets, is problematic. Thus, extending the obligation to 'indefinitely' creates a potential legal contradiction because Delaware law may view such a duration as an unreasonable restraint, particularly if it covers more than just trade secrets. The original four-year limit could be seen as a reasonable balance, but the indefinite term removes this balance, potentially making the clause unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that it will not apply for or obtain any intellectual property protection in respect of the other Party’s Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that it will not apply for or obtain any intellectual property protection in respect of the other Party’s Information, even if such information is independently developed.
perturbation.explanation: This clause now prevents a party from obtaining IP protection on independently developed technology, even if that technology is only similar to the other party's information. This could violate federal patent law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal patent law allows inventors to patent new and non-obvious inventions. If a party independently develops an invention, even if it relates to information disclosed by the other party (but not directly derived from it), preventing them from seeking patent protection conflicts with the inventor's rights under 35 U.S.C. § 102, which defines the conditions for patentability. The 'even if such information is independently developed' language expands the scope of the restriction to be overbroad, thereby conflicting with this federal law.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text expands the restriction on intellectual property protection, preventing a party from obtaining IP protection even on independently developed technology similar to the other party's information. The Law Explanation states this conflicts with inventor's rights under 35 U.S.C. § 102, because it restricts them from seeking patent protection for independently developed inventions. Snippet 2 mentions 35 U.S.C. 102(a) and (b), which supports the Law Explanation that the change might violate patent law. The original contract only restricted parties from obtaining IP on the other party's information, but the modification creates a legal contradiction.
